event: 71 years old
female
history of hypertension
history of ischaemic heart disease
history of peripheral vascular disease
brought to emergency room in unconscious state
stroke
head computed tomographic scan
condition deteriorated rapidly
requiring inotropic support
septic shock
elevated levels of inflammatory markers
erythrocyte sedimentation rate, 70 mm/h
C-reactive protein, 78 mg/L
blood obtained at this time grew a yeast
aerobic and anaerobic BacT/ALERT culture bottles
caspofungin was administered immediately
died on day 3 of her hospital stay
yeast isolate (Kw553/18)
identified as C. parapsilosis by the VITEK 2 yeast identification system
referred to the Mycology Reference Laboratory for further characterization
produced turquoise blue colonies on CHROMagar Candida
formed long ellipsoidal-shaped ascospores on acetate ascospore agar
identity as L. elongisporus
internally transcribed spacer region of ribosomal DNA was amplified and sequenced
DNA sequence data comparisons of Kw553/18 showed complete (100%) identity with the corresponding sequence from L. elongisporus type strain
no apparent risk factors in the patient when fungaemia was diagnosed
developed fungaemia as a result of this unusual yeast in the absence of any known risk factors
possibility of inoculation of the yeast from the skin or translocation from the gastrointestinal tract cannot be ruled out
L. elongisporus is a recognized bloodstream pathogen
little is known about its virulence attributes or its environmental niche
capable of causing diverse clinical conditions, including endocarditis
global prevalence
isolated from patients in distant geographic regions
antifungal susceptibility was determined by Etest
MIC values (μg/mL) were as follows: amphotericin B, 0.012; fluconazole, 0.125; voriconazole, 0.004; posaconazole, 0.003; itraconazole, 0.008; flucytosine, 0.064; caspofungin, 0.064; micafungin, 0.003
unusual aspect of our case is that there were no apparent risk factors in the patient when fungaemia was diagnosed
she had a history of heart disease and had also experienced a stroke
she was not receiving any antibiotics
nor had any central lines been emplaced
she was hospitalized earlier for lower limb ischaemia due to peripheral vascular disease
she was discharged 2 weeks before the current episode
it is not clear how she developed fungaemia as a result of this unusual yeast in the absence of any known risk factors
the possibility of inoculation of the yeast from the skin or translocation from the gastrointestinal tract cannot be ruled out
